coredata.dc:title: Boosting lactic acid photocatalytic synthesis from biomass sugars: The synergistic effect of N-doped carbon dots on ultrathin carbon nitride
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Xiao
Wang, Xin
He, Yiyang
Li, Jixin
Yang, Haiyan
Zhang, Pingping
Yang, Songrui
Yu, Haixiang
Xu, Suyun
Yuan, Haiping
Gu, Lin
coredata.dc:description: The green and sustainable production of lactic acid via photocatalytic conversion of biomass-derived sugars is highly significant owing to its enhanced efficiency and reduced energy requirements. Consequently, the investigation has engineered a metal-free photocatalyst (NCDs/CCN), consisting of N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and ultrathin carbon nitride (CCN). This catalyst has an enhanced light absorption range, facilitating a marked acceleration in the separation rate of photogenerated carriers. It has demonstrated the capability to achieve a lactic acid yield of up to 87.6 % in just 90 min with a mere 20 mg catalyst concentration in a xylose-alkali system. Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR) and quenching experiments indicate that superoxide radicals (·O2 −) are the primary oxidizing active species in the photocatalytic system, followed by h+, ·OH, and 1O2. DFT analysis suggests nitrogen doping enhances interaction with xylose, lowering adsorption energy and accelerating lactic acid generation, thus improving economic feasibility and sustainability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrathin carbon nitride
N-doped carbon dot
Lactic acid
Biomass sugars
Photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of conditions on xylose photocatalytic lactic acid synthesis using NCDs/CCN
Effects of different oxidation species on photocatalytic lactic acid synthesis
The comparison of different catalysts for lactic acid production
Role of NCDs on enhanced photocatalytic production of lactic acid by CCN
Possible mechanism for xylose photocatalytic conversion by NCDs/CCN

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of BCN (a), NCDs/BCN (b), NCDs/CCN (c). SEM images of BCN (d), NCDs/BCN (e), NCDs/CCN (f). The mapping of C, N, O in NCDs/CCN (g), HRTEM image of NCDs/CCN (h).
(a) XRD patterns, (b) FTIR spectra of catalysts. XPS spectra of BCN, NCDs/BCN and NCDs/CCN: (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s. (e) UV–vis absorption spectra, (f) plot of (αhv)1/2 versus light energy.
UPS with cut-off and secondary cut-off of BCN (a, d), NCDs/BCN (b, e) and NCDs/CCN (c, f).

The effects of various conditions on lactic acid synthesis: (a) Catalyst dosage, (b) KOH solution concentration, (c) Reaction temperature, (d) Reaction time, (e) NCDs/CCN photocatalyst recycling, (f) Selective oxidation of biomass-based monosaccharides to lactic acid by NCDs/CCN.

EPR spectra of (a) DMPO-·OH, (b) DMPO-·O2 −, (c) TEMPONE-1O2, (d) TEMPO-h+, (e) TEMPO-e− in different systems and (f) the impact of various oxidation active species on the synthesis of lactic acid from xylose.

(a, b) Top and (c, d) Side perspectives of the optimal configurations for a xylose molecule adsorbed onto BCN and NCDs/CCN, (e, f) Density of states, (g) Structure optimization, (h) Electrostatic potential, (i) HOMO, LUMO and (j) Calculated Fukui-index of xylose.
Potential pathways for xylose photocatalytic reforming via NCDs/CCN under visible light.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of NCDs/CCN.
The effects of different photocatalysts on lactic acid synthesis under different conditions.
Calculated adsorption energies of a xylose molecule on the NCDs, BCN and NCDs/CCN.

head.ce:title: Boosting lactic acid photocatalytic synthesis from biomass sugars: The synergistic effect of N-doped carbon dots on ultrathin carbon nitride

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment and Architecture, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai 200093, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment and Architecture, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200093, P.R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, 200240, P.R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The green and sustainable production of lactic acid via photocatalytic conversion of biomass-derived sugars is highly significant owing to its enhanced efficiency and reduced energy requirements. Consequently, the investigation has engineered a metal-free photocatalyst (NCDs/CCN), consisting of N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and ultrathin carbon nitride (CCN). This catalyst has an enhanced light absorption range, facilitating a marked acceleration in the separation rate of photogenerated carriers. It has demonstrated the capability to achieve a lactic acid yield of up to 87.6 % in just 90 min with a mere 20 mg catalyst concentration in a xylose-alkali system. Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR) and quenching experiments indicate that superoxide radicals (·O2 −) are the primary oxidizing active species in the photocatalytic system, followed by h+, ·OH, and 1O2. DFT analysis suggests nitrogen doping enhances interaction with xylose, lowering adsorption energy and accelerating lactic acid generation, thus improving economic feasibility and sustainability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: As a multifunctional compound, lactic acid (LA), also known as 2-hydroxypropionic acid (CH3CHOHCOOH), finds extensive applications across food, medicine, cosmetics, and the manufacturing of biodegradable plastics. The presence of an asymmetric carbon atom in lactic acid molecules enables the formation of two optical isomers, L (+) lactic acid and D (−) lactic acid, which exhibit differing activities and functionalities (Derabli et al., 2022). In food production, lactic acid serves multiple roles, functioning as an acidifier, flavoring agent, antimicrobial agent, and preservative (Yang et al., 2019a). Its unique microbial inhibition properties place lactic acid at the forefront of food preservation and processing technologies. Furthermore, as a precursor for biodegradable polymers, polylactic acid derivatives demonstrate significant potential in the active food packaging industry by ensuring food quality, extending shelf life, and enhancing sensory and safety performance (Altan, Aytac, & Uyar, 2018).

By 2025, due to the growing interest in eco-friendly products derived from lactic acid, global demand for lactic acid is expected to increase to 1960.1 thousand tons (Alves de Oliveira, Komesu, Vaz Rossell, & Maciel Filho, 2018). Currently, the industrial-scale production of lactic acid primarily involves two methods: biological fermentation and chemical synthesis. Nonetheless, biofermentation is marked by extended fermentation durations, stringent operational demands, and challenges associated with the purification and concentration of the product, elevating the costs associated with downstream processing. In contrast, chemical synthesis is distinguished by its efficiency, precision, and product purity, offering notable advantages (Ling et al., 2023). For example, Meng et al. (Xia, Shen, Xiao, Gu, & Zhang, 2023) have prepared a multilevel bimetallic (Mg-Sn-Beta-H) zeolite catalyst with multiple active sites and a hierarchical structure, which enhances the conversion of glucose to lactic acid to a yield of 62.7 % under conditions of 190 °C for 2 h. Although chemical synthesis can efficiently produce lactic acid, the process inevitably involves high energy consumption and relatively high production costs due to the required high temperatures and pressures. Consequently, the development of efficient and energy-conserving synthesis methods remains a pivotal area of research focus.

Biomass-derived sugars, serving as renewable sources of organic carbon, are capable of being transformed under specified conditions into chemicals and fuels of high value (Mbiankeu Nguea & Hervé Kaffo, 2024), such as biodiesel (Ao et al., 2024), 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) (Zhao et al., 2022), lactic acid (Ma et al., 2021a), and gluconic acid (Zhang, Wan, Yu, & Tsang, 2021). Commonly, a variety of sugars undergo microbial fermentation, resulting in the production of lactic acid and its derivatives. Beyond this traditional method, the catalytic transformation of sugars into derivatives of lactic acid has emerged as a significant area of research interest in recent years. Photocatalysis has emerged as a leading method in the realm of catalysis, with recent research gaining significant attention due to its harnessing of clean and renewable energy sources. Employing solar energy presents abundant advantages including high efficiency, mild reaction conditions, and environmental sustainability (Ouyang et al., 2022), thus facilitating green and sustainable chemical processes. It is believed that photocatalysis would offer a potent solution to the challenges inherent in conventional lactic acid production methodologies.

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), a non-metallic semiconductor, consists exclusively of carbon and nitrogen atoms. It features an extensive conjugated π-system based on sp2 hybridization and demonstrates exceptional responsiveness to visible light (absorption peak around 450–460 nm), with a bandgap of approximately 2.7 eV (Hu et al., 2019). The materials exhibits robust resistance to acids, alkalis, and photodegradation, providing dependable stability, with its structure and properties readily adaptable. However, the rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs, along with its low surface area and insufficient light absorption, limits the practical application of g-C3N4 during the photocatalytic production of lactic acid (Mo et al., 2022). Ling et al. (2023) reported that under under illumination conditions at 40 °C, the lactic acid yield from the selective oxidation of xylose by pristine CNs photocatalysis was only 46.1 % within 4 h.

Compared to bulk g-C3N4, ultra-thin g-C3N4, with its augmented specific surface area and intensified interfacial interactions, coupled with its nanoscale thickness (<5 nm), significantly bolsters photocatalytic efficiency by condensing the electron transport pathway and diminishing carrier recombination rates (Huang et al., 2023). However, the weak electron coupling brought by the conjugated sp2 structures great limit the electron mobility of g-C3N4. To enhance the performance of ultrathin g-C3N4 (CCN), various techniques have been suggested to improve its photocatalytic activity, such as element doping, constructing heterostructures, surface modification, and coupling with semiconductor materials (Wang & Wang, 2022). For exemple Ma et al. (2021b) developed a nitrogen-doped carbon photocatalyst with a controllable carbon/nitrogen (C/N) ratio, which enhanced the yield of lactic acid to 76.82 % under visible light irradiation at 60 °C. Lv et al. (2023) developed a composite material consisting of boron-doped carbon nitride supported on cuprous oxide (g-C3N4- B-Cu2O). Under illumination at 60 °C for 120 min, the catalyst achieved a xylose conversion rate of 99.3 %, with the lactic acid yield peaking at 91.2 %. However, metal doping inevitably cause metal leaching and increase the cost of treatment (Kim & Jo, 2020). Therefore, it is of great interest to search for green, sustainable, and catalytically promising non-metallic materials for g-C3N4 modification.

Carbon dots (CDs) are typically nanostructured carbon materials formed by the aggregation of carbon atoms, presenting spherical or rod-like structures with sizes typically ranging from 1 to 10 nm (F. X. Zhao et al., 2023). They possess unique properties such as small size, non-toxicity, excellent optical performance, and biocompatibility, which can be obtained from biomass (Y. Wang et al., 2023). CDs possess a π-conjugated system that forms a band structure of electronic energy levels within the carbon dots, resulting in electron excitation and transition processes. Notably, due to the high electronegativity of nitrogen atoms, incorporating N atoms into CDs can adjust the band structure and energy levels (Shang, Liu, Chen, & Wang, 2023). This enables CDs to exhibit a wider range of optical response and enhanced light absorption capacity. Moreover, the integration of N-CDs into CCN has generated significant interest. The CDs introduces additional active sites and functional groups, potentially endowing CCN with new electronic properties. This provides a perspective to enhance the catalytic performance of CCN by introducing delocalization of aromatic π-conjugated planner, resulting in a greatly improved charge transfer between NCDs/CCN and reactant. The dual role of CDs with both catalytic sites and adsorption sites for biomass sugars, minimizing the distance of reactive oxidative species to the reactant. To our knowledge, the coupling of N-CQDs and ultra-thin g-C3N4 as catalyts for enhanced photocatalytic synthesis of lactic acid have not been explored.

Herein, this study utilized a hydrothermal method followed by a straightforward secondary calcination process to synthesize N-doped carbon dots incorporated within ultra-thin g-C3N4 (NCDs/CCN). The catalyst was successfully employed for the photocatalytic selective synthesis of lactic acid from various C5 and C6 biomass sugars. The dual effect of NCDs on promoting the efficient adsorption and photocatalytic conversion of sugars was well presented. Characterization was carried out to explore the morphological structure and photoelectrochemical properties of different photocatalysts. Active species generated by NCDs-CCN were further explored using EPR spectroscopy to conduct trapping experiments, aiming to elucidate the photocatalytic mechanism in depth. Finally, the potential route for the selective oxidation synthesis of lactic acid from biomass sugars was revealed through density functional theory (DFT) and LC-MS analysis.

Ethylenediamine (EDA, 99 %), citric acid (CA, 98 %), Glucose (C6H12O6, 96 %), xylose (C5H10O5, ≥99 %), arabinose (C5H10O5, 98 %), fructose (C6H12O6, 99 %), rhamnose (C6H12O5·H2O, 99 %), mannose (C6H12O6, 99 %), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (C10H16N2O8, 99.5 %), tryptophan (C11H12N2O2, 98 %), p-benzoquinone (C6H4O2, 98 %), isopropyl (C3H8O, ≥99.9 %), potassium hydroxide (KOH, 95 %), 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl (TEMPO), 4-oxo-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEPONE) and 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Urea (CH4N2O, 99.0 %), lactic acid (C3H6O3, 90 %), formic acid (CH2O2, 99 %), Acetic acid (CH3COOH, 99.8 %), were supplied from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. All materials or chemicals used were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

Citric acid (5 mmol) and ethylenediamine (335 uL) were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water, the mixture was subjected to a hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 5 h, followed by freeze-drying to obtain NCDs.

The NCDs/BCN composites were synthesized via a thermal polymerization method. The main steps involved mixing urea (3 g) with a certain mass of NCDs (x mg, where x = 3, 5, 9 mg) to achieve a uniform mixture, then transfer it to the crucible. The crucible was placed in a muffle furnace and heated to 550 °C for 3 h. The resulting samples were labeled as NCDs/BCN-x (x = 3, 5, 9). The synthesis method for BCN (graphitic carbon nitride) was the same as for NCDs/BCN-x, except that NCDs were not included. The materials prepared in the previous step were calcined in a tube furnace under N2 atmosphere at 600 °C for 2 h at a ramp rate of 10 °C/min to obtain CCN and NCDs/CCN-x (x = 3, 5, 9). (See Scheme 1.)
The technical means of characterization of materials are described in Text. S1.

100 mg of xylose and a calculated amount of photocatalyst (NCDs/CCN) were introduced into KOH solutions of various concentrations. The mixture was left in the dark for 30 min before undergoing a photocatalytic reaction under visible light irradiation (300 W) at different temperatures for a designated period. After the reaction, the samples were directly analyzed using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Text. S2). Under the optimized conditions, the universality of photocatalytic biomass sugar conversion was investigated by replacing xylose with sugars containing 5–6 carbon atoms such as fructose, arabinose, glucose, and mannose.

As in conventional preparation, the catalyst was used for the photocatalytic conversion of biomass sugars to lactic acid. Post-reaction, the catalyst was subjected to centrifugation, followed by filtration, and rinsed with deionized water until it reached a neutral pH level. It was then dried at 60 °C for reuse in subsequent cycles. The reaction products were analyzed directly using HPLC.

To assess the impact of various active agents within the system, a series of ROS scavenging experiment were performed. Initially, a mixture containing 100 mg of xylose and 20 mg of NCDs/CCN, along with different sacrificial agents (p-BQ, TBA, KI, and L-histidine), was prepared in 20 mL of a 2 mol/L KOH solution. This mixture was left in darkness for 30 min, then exposed to visible light at 50 °C for 90 min. After irradiation, the conversion of xylose and the yield of lactic acid were quantitatively analyzed using HPLC.

The morphology and microstructure of BCN, CDs/BCN and NCDs/CCN were analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis methods. The original g-C3N4 (BCN) possesses a dense layered structure (Fig. 1a and d). There is no significant change in the morphology of NCDs/BCN and NCDs/CCN, but noticeable black spots can be observed (Fig. 1b and e, c and f). Additionally, as observed through HRTEM (Fig. 1h), these black spots exhibit a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the interplanar spacing of (100) plane for graphitic carbon (Shang, Liu, Chen, & Wang, 2023). This observation confirms the successful coupling of NCDs with BCN and CCN. It's worth noting that after the secondary calcination, NCDs/CCN are peeled into sheet-like ultrathin nanosheets, and a porous structure is observed (Fig. 1c). The thickness of the nanosheets was investigated using AFM. (Fig. S1), the thickness of the NCDs/CCN nanosheets is approximately 2.56 nm, which is consistent with the TEM results. It is hypothesized that the ultrathin nanolayer structure helps to prevent the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thereby enhancing the efficiency of charge carrier separation (Wang et al., 2017).

The crystal structures and chemical characteristics of the nanosheet samples, including BCN, NCDs/BCN, and NCDs/CCN, were analyzed using XRD patterns and FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. 2a illustrates the XRD patterns for BCN, NCDs/BCN, and NCDs/CCN. Each sample displayed distinct diffraction peaks at 12.9° and 27.5°, which are associated with the (100) and (002) planes of g-C3N4, respectively. These peaks denote the in-plane structural organization of tri-s-triazine units and the stacking of aromatic layers (Jin et al., 2021). Compared to BCN, following the loading of NCDs, both NCDs/BCN and NCDs/CCN exhibit a decreased angle in the (002) diffraction peak, indicating that the loading of NCDs has increased the interlayer spacing of BCN. The FTIR spectra for BCN, NCDs/BCN, and NCDs/CCN, depicted in Fig. 2b, show distinctive absorption peaks. These occur at 806 cm−1, between 1200 and 1700 cm−1, and from 3000 to 3400 cm−1, representing the vibrational modes of triazine units, CN heterocyclic stretches, and the stretches of OH and NH bonds, respectively (Li et al., 2022b). In addition, the intensity of the CH peak at 2800 cm−1 in NCDs/CCN is significantly enhanced, further confirming that NCDs have been doped into the carbon nitride (Liu et al., 2024b; Slewa, 2024).

Fig. S2 presents the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of various catalysts. BCN, NCDs/BCN and NCDs/CCN all have mesoporous structures. The formation of abundant porosity on the surface is attributed to the substantial release of gases during the pyrolysis of NCDs and nitrogen-doped carbon. NCDs/CCN possesses a specific surface area of 84.75 m2/g, which is significantly higher than that of NCDs/BCN (82.10 m2/g) and BCN (56.15 m2/g) (Table S1), suggesting that secondary calcination can introduce additional active sites, thereby enhancing photocatalytic activity (Jiao, Li, Wang, He, & Li, 2020; Jin et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2019b).

The chemical compositions and binding states of BCN, NCDs/BCN, and NCDs/CCN were further examined by XPS. In the full spectrum of XPS, it can be observed that all catalyst materials contain C, N, O elements (Fig. S3). The C 1 s spectrum of BCN in Fig. 2c can be divided into two peaks centered at 284.80 eV and 288.33 eV, corresponding to CC/CC and NCH, respectively (Jin et al., 2021). Upon doping carbon dots and subjecting them to secondary calcination, a new peak emerged at 286.14 eV, corresponding to CO/CO, in both NCDs/BCN and NCDs/CCN. This occurrence is attributed to the formation of a conjugated structure between NCDs and BCN, resulting in the hydroxylation of carbon atoms on the surface of the material. The enhanced hydroxyl (C-OH) peak is attributed to the adsorption of H2O molecules on the NCDs surface and the oxygen-containing functional groups of dots (Jin et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2019; Si et al., 2021). In comparison to BCN, it was observed that in NCDs/CCN, the peak associated with NCN shifted in the direction of lower binding energy by 0.22 eV. This variation indicates that the ultra-thin sheet-like structure facilitates the movement of electrons within NCDs and CCN. This behavior is associated with the presence of pyrrole-N in NCDs. Pyrrole-N acts as an electron-donating moiety, facilitating electron transfer from nitrogen to carbon, thereby adjusting the electron distribution within NCDs, consequently enhancing electron transport properties (Fig. S4). The N 1 s spectra (Fig. 2d) were fitted with four peaks, corresponding to different nitrogen species: sp2-hybridized nitrogen atoms attached to carbon in the aromatic ring (CNC), which represent the basic carbon‑nitrogen heterocyclic unit of BCN; bridging nitrogen atoms in N(C)3 (NC3); amino functional groups (CNH); and oxidized nitrogen (ON). Compared with BCN, the N 1 s and C 1 s peaks of NCDs/BCN and NCDs/CCN shifted significantly, indicating the presence of interfacial interactions between NCDs and g-C3N4. These interactions may lead to the redistribution of electrons and changes in binding energies, thus affecting the photoelectrical properties of the catalyst (Zhang et al., 2022b).

The optical absorption properties of the photocatalysts BCN, NCDs/BCN, and NCDs/CCN were analyzed by UV–vis DRS. As depicted in Fig. 2e, the BCN exhibited an absorption edge at 446 nm. For the modified samples, the absorption edges shifted progressively to 451 nm for NCDs/BCN and further to 456 nm for NCDs/CCN. This indicates that NCDs/BCN and NCDs/CCN exhibit stronger absorption intensity and a broader absorption range compared to BCN, which can be attributed to the upconversion photoluminescent properties of NCDs, enhancing the photocatalytic efficiency of the composite catalyst. It further confirms the successful loading of NCDs onto carbon nitride (Chen et al., 2021). g-C3N4 as a typical direct bandgap semiconductor (n = 1), can have its bandgap calculated using the Tau equation (Eq. 1). The results indicate that the bandgaps of BCN, NCDs/BCN, and NCDs/CCN are approximately 2.58 eV, 2.49 eV, and 2.47 eV, respectively (Fig. 2f). Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) is used to measure the valence band (VB), while the work function (Φ) is determined by subtracting the cutoff energy from the excitation energy of ultraviolet light (21.20 eV) (Liu et al., 2024a). Therefore, the Φ values for BCN, NCDs/BCN and NCDs/CCN are 2.58 eV, 2.49 eV, and 2.47 eV, respectively. Additionally, according to Eq. (2), the VB can be obtained using Φ and the secondary electron cutoff (ESECO) which is shown in Fig. 3 . The VB of BCN, NCDs/BCN, and NCDs/CCN were found to be −6.55, −6.40, and − 6.25 eV vs. vacuum. For ease of comparison, the reference values should be converted to the NHE scale by using Eq. (3), where 4.44 represents the difference between the vacuum level and NHE (Li, Ma, Hong, & Sun, 2024). Therefore, the VB values for BCN, NCDs/BCN, and NCDs/CCN is determined to be 2.11, 1.96, and 1.81 V vs. NHE. The band structure diagrams of the photocatalysts are shown in Fig. S5b according to Eq. 4. These results suggest that doping g-C3N4 with NCDs changes the bandgap and further enhances the photocatalytic performance.
(1)

= A
hϑ − Eg

− VB = Φ +
E SECO

E g

The dynamics of photogenerated carrier separation and transfer in the synthesized photocatalysts were analyzed through photoluminescence (PL), photocurrent measurements, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Fig. S6a shows that both NCDs/BCN and NCDs/CCN exhibit reduced PL intensities compared to BCN, suggesting diminished recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. This reduction is attributed to the charge transfer capabilities of the NCDs and their ultrathin structures, which effectively inhibit carrier recombination and enhance separation efficiency. Subsequently, TRPL spectra were used to investigate the lifetime of photoexcited charge carriers. As show in the Fig. S6b and Table S2, the average lifetime of NCDs/CCN is 3.902 ns much lower than that of BCN (4.473 ns), which further demonstrates electron transfer in NCDs/CCN. Fig. S6c shows the EIS of BCN, NCDs/BCN, and NCDs/CCN. It can be observed that the Nyquist arc radius of NCDs/CCN is smaller than that of BCN and NCDs/BCN. The Nyquist arc radius of NCDs/CCN is the smallest, indicating the highest charge transfer efficiency (Li et al., 2022a). Moreover, NCDs/CCN showed a stronger photocurrent response (Fig. S6d), indicating the superior charge separation ability of the catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of conditions on xylose photocatalytic lactic acid synthesis using NCDs/CCN
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic efficacy of catalyst materials prepared under varying conditions for the xylose-alkali system was investigated using catalysts BCN, NCDs/BCN and NCDs/CCN, as shown in Fig. 4 . Initial experiments involved the photocatalytic conversion of xylose (100 mg) in the presence of a catalyst (20 mg) and KOH solution (10 mL, 2 mol/L), under visible light irradiation at 50 °C for 90 min. Minimal lactic acid production was observed with BCN, indicating limited activity. However, upon doping with NCDs, the lactic acid yield from NCDs/BCN significantly increased to 66.5 %. A further enhancement to 87.6 % in lactic acid yield was achieved with catalyst NCDs/CCN, following a secondary calcination of the composite catalyst. These results unequivocally demonstrate the superior photocatalytic performance of NCDs/CCN in the conversion of xylose to lactic acid, positioning it as an efficacious photocatalyst for such transformations.

To explore the impact of various conditions on the photocatalytic synthesis of lactic acid by NCDs/CCN. The effect of different KOH concentrations on the yield of lactic acid was initially examined (Fig. 5a). An enhancement in lactic acid production was observed with an increase in KOH concentration from 0.5 to 2.0 mol/L, elevating yields from 51.8 % to 87.6 %. Subsequent increases in KOH concentration resulted in a decline in lactic acid yield, attributed to the proliferation of oxidizing species at higher alkali concentrations leading to lactic acid overoxidation. Meanwhile, the conversion rates of xylose improved, leading to the generation of byproducts including formic acid and acetic acid. The catalyst dosage played a pivotal role in lactic acid formation, as depicted in Fig. 5b, where lactic acid yield exhibited a volcanic trend with an increase in catalyst dosage from 5 to 30 mg, potentially due to catalyst agglomeration inducing light scattering (Ling et al., 2023). An optimal catalyst dosage of 20 mg was identified, achieving the highest lactic acid yield of 87.6 %. Additionally, an increase in catalyst dosage correlated with enhanced xylose conversion rates and the concurrent generation of formic acid.

In addition, the temperature at which the reaction occurs plays a crucial role in xylose conversion. Consequently, studies were conducted to examine how varying reaction temperatures affect the selective oxidation of xylose in the production of lactic acid, as depicted in Fig. 5c. The increase of temperature can accelerate the reaction rate and increase the conversion of xylose, so we will find that the conversion of xylose increases continuously under the temperature gradient from 30 to 70 °C. However, the yield of lactic acid peaks at 60 °C with a maximum of 87.6 % and starts to decrease above 60 °C. The reason for this phenomenon is that lactic acid is easily converted to other by-products at high temperatures.

Furthermore, the duration of the reaction notably impacts the production of lactic acid from xylose. The yield of lactic acid, as shown in Fig. 5d, initially increased and then decreased as the exposure to light was extended, peaking at 90 min. However, an extended reaction time may result in the degradation of lactic acid and increased operating costs. With longer light exposure, the xylose conversion rate increased to 100 %. Likewise, the formation of byproducts such as formic acid and acetic acid was noted during these reactions.

To assess the photocatalyst's broad applicability for converting biomass sugars, we systematically analyzed the conversion rates and lactic acid production from five monosaccharides: Xylose, Fructose, Arabinose, Glucose, and Mannose. Results depicted in Fig. 5e reveal conversion efficiencies of 99.6 % for Xylose, 93.2 % for Fructose, 95.8 % for Arabinose, 96.5 % for Glucose, and 82.7 % for Mannose, alongside lactic acid yields of 86.7 %, 74.2 %, 70.5 %, 76.4 %, and 61.7 % for each monosaccharide, respectively. Notably, Xylose and Arabinose, classified as pentoses, exhibited slightly superior lactic acid yields compared to the hexoses—Fructose, Glucose, and Mannose. This observation underscores the efficacy of the photocatalyst across both pentose and hexose sugars, demonstrating its versatile applicability in biomass sugar conversion.

The stability and regenerative capability of photocatalysts are essential for the practical implementation of photocatalytic oxidation technology. This study investigates the stability of composite NCDs/CCN photocatalysts in the conversion of xylose to lactic acid. Experiments were conducted under optimal conditions (xylose concentration of 10 mg/mL, KOH concentration of 2.0 mol/L, catalyst dosage of 20 mg, reaction temperature of 60 °C, and duration of 90 min). Post-reaction, xylose conversion and lactic acid yield were analyzed via HPLC. The photocatalysts were then recovered, rinsed with deionized water, and dried at 55 °C for reuse in subsequent experiments. After five cycles of reuse, the results are displayed in Fig. 5f. The conversion rate of xylose remained largely constant, while the yield of lactic acid gradually decreased with the increasing number of cycles the catalyst was reused. The lactic acid yield dropped from 87.6 % to 74.8 %, representing a loss of approximately 12.8 %. This reduction in yield might be linked to the buildup of humic substances on the catalyst's surface throughout the reaction. Such accumulation impedes adequate contact between the catalyst's active sites and the substrate, leading to a noticeable reduction in lactic acid production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of different oxidation species on photocatalytic lactic acid synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: To identify active substances during the reaction process and further investigate the catalyst mechanism, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) characterization was performed. Three capture agents were used to trap photo-generated electrons (e−), holes (h+), superoxide radicals (·O2 −), hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and singlet oxygen (1O2). For ·OH and ·O2 −, DMPO (5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide) can directly detect the signal peaks of DMPO-·O2 − and DMPO-·OH. TEMPONE (4-oxo-2,2,6,6- tetramethylpiperidine) is used as a spin trap agent to detect 1O2 (Li, Ma, Hong, & Sun, 2024). Furthermore, TEMPO (2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-N-oxyl) can be used to directly detect e− and h+ in the system. When TEMPO reacts with e− and h+, it is consumed, and its signal decreases. As depicted in Fig. 6a, when ·OH radicals were detected with DMPO under various conditions, no significant peaks were observed in the absence of both catalyst and illumination. Upon the introduction of NCDs/CCN, a signal peak with a DMPO-·OH ratio of 1:2:2:1 was detected, confirming the generation of high levels of oxidative radicals following the addition of the catalyst. Under identical conditions, the ·O2 − radicals exhibit the same trend across different systems, with the sole distinction being that the DMPO-·O2 − displays a quadruplet spectrum of equal intensity (Fig. 6b). In the similar verification strategy, TEMPONE was used to instead of DMPO as the spin trapping to explore the generation of 1O2. As shown in Fig. 6c, no signals were observed in systems containing only the catalyst but without TEMPONE. Upon addition of TEMPONE and NCDs/CCN, three signals with equal intensity of 1:1:1 emerged, indicating the generation of 1O2. Moreover, the intensity in the NCDs/CCN system was greater than that in the NCDs/BCN system and BCN system.

Furthermore, TEMPO was employed to capture the generated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) on the photocatalyst surface. As shown in Fig. 6d, the EPR spectra exhibited a triplet peak with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1 under both NCDs/CCN + dark conditions and in the absence of a catalyst under illumination. However, the signal intensity of TEMPO decreased significantly in the NCDs/CCN + visible light system, indicating the generation of h+. It is worth noting that e− and h+ exist in pairs on the photocatalyst surface, thus the change in the e− signal is similar to that of h+ (Fig. 6e) (Liu et al., 2024a).

Based on the aforementioned events, free radical quenching experiments were carried out in the presence of NCDs/CCN + KOH + Xylose system. Tert-butanol (TBA) was widely regarded to quench ·OH, p-BQ was invested to quench ·O2 −, potassium iodide (KI) and L-histidine were used as scavengers of h+ and 1O2, respectively (Ma et al., 2021a). As shown in Fig. 6f, upon the addition of various quenchers in the NCDs/CCN catalyst system, a decrease in lactic acid yield was observed, among which p-BQ exhibited the strongest inhibitory effect on the photocatalytic conversion process, followed by L-histidine, TBA, and KI. This indicates that ·O2 − plays the most crucial role throughout the photocatalytic process, while 1O2, ·OH, and h+ also contribute to a certain extent in promoting the reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The comparison of different catalysts for lactic acid production
ce:section.ce:para: To delineate the advantages of NCDs/CCN catalysts, a comparative study was conducted, referencing diverse catalysts from the literature for lactic acid synthesis, while systematically varying photocatalyst concentrations. As depicted in Table 1 , the synthesis of lactic acid from xylose in most photocatalytic systems necessitated stringent conditions, notably higher catalyst doses and prolonged reaction times (entries 2–5). Furthermore, despite the photocatalytic conversion processes for glucose and xylose sharing an identical reaction duration of 90 min, the economic considerations of employing a high concentration of catalyst are substantial, as indicated by the data presented in entries 6 and 8, our catalyst—termed NCDs/CCN—demonstrates a distinct advantage in terms of functionality and efficiency. It not only achieves comparable conversion rates but also does so with significantly lower catalyst concentrations and reduced reaction times, thereby minimizing both time and energy consumption. This performance sets NCDs/CCN apart from the vast majority of catalysts reported in the literature, underscoring its potential as a superior alternative for the photocatalytic synthesis of lactic acid from renewable feedstocks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of NCDs on enhanced photocatalytic production of lactic acid by CCN
ce:section.ce:para: The role of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) in enhancing the photocatalytic production of lactic acid by CCN materials can be understood through DFT calculations. Firstly, we performed computational calculations to determine the adsorption energy of xylose molecules onto the surfaces of both BCN and NCDs/CCN. The results, as illustrated in Fig. 7 , Fig. S8 and detailed in Table 2 , reveal that the adsorption energy of xylose on material BCN is −7.52 eV, and on material NCDs it is −1.26 eV. Clearly, the adsorption energy of NCDs/CCN is more negative, at −7.74 eV, indicating that the composite material of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and ultrathin graphitic carbon nitride can significantly enhance the adsorption performance for xylose. This greater magnitude of the adsorption energy for NCDs/CCN implies a stronger interaction between the xylose molecules and the NCDs/CCN surface. It is postulated that the carbonyl (CO) functional group of xylose exhibits a higher affinity for adsorption onto the NCDs/CCN surface, potentially due to the enhanced electronic properties and the presence of more active adsorption sites on the NCDs/CCN. The preferential adsorption sites for molecules on the surfaces of BCN and NCDs/CCN are distinct, a phenomenon that can be ascribed to the significant disparities in the local electrostatic potentials. These variations arise from the introduction of NCDs, which alter the electronic landscape of the material, facilitates the adsorption process, and stabilizes the molecular geometry upon adsorption (Ma et al., 2021a).

To better understand the electronic structure, we calculated the density of states (DOS) for BCN and NCDs/CCN. The DOS results elucidate that the valence orbitals of BCN and NCDs/CCN are predominantly constituted by the 2p orbitals of carbon and nitrogen, along with contributions from the 2 s orbitals of nitrogen (Fig. 7e, f). Notably, in comparison to BCN, NCDs/CCN exhibited a pronounced electron density in the vicinity of the Fermi level. This enhancement can be predominantly ascribed to the novel electronic properties that NCDs introduce to the g-C3N4 framework. The observation is further corroborated by the charge density mapping of the composite material (Fig. S7b). In the case of NCDs/CCN, the map delineates the significant accumulation of charge density around the Fermi level, which is a direct consequence of the electronic interactions between the incorporated NCDs and the carbon and nitrogen co-doped matrix. This enhanced electron density is indicative of the improved photocatalytic potential of NCDs/CCN, suggesting a more efficient separation of photogenerated charge carriers and a higher propensity for redox reactions, which are essential for the production of lactic acid. Therefore, NCDs as electron transport channels, can facilitate the generation of more active species such as ·O2 −. Furthermore, the ultrathin g-C3N4 effectively improves photocatalytic performance by shortening the electron transfer path and reducing the recombination rate of carriers.

Following the structural optimization of xylose molecules, the electrostatic potential, HOMO and LUMO orbitals, and Fukui indices of xylose molecules were calculated to further determine the reactive sites and validate the reaction pathway of photocatalytic conversion of xylose to lactic acid (Text. S3). The electrostatic potential of xylose molecules reveals electron-rich areas in blue and electron-deficient, nucleophilic areas in red. Generally, functional groups associated with the HOMO are prone to electrophilic reactions, while those associated with the LUMO are susceptible to nucleophilic reactions (Yang et al., 2022). As demonstrated in Fig. 6h 6i and 6j, various reactive species typically attack the CO site of xylose molecules. To further validate the active sites, the Fukui index of xylose molecules was calculated, where f−, f0, and f+ represent the indices for electrophilic, radical, and nucleophilic attacks, respectively. It is observed that O6 exhibits strong electrophilicity and radical reactivity, making it susceptible to attacks by electrophilic species such as h+, 1O2, and ·OH, while C1 displays significant nucleophilicity, making it a target for ·O2 − attacks (P. Chen et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for xylose photocatalytic conversion by NCDs/CCN
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on experimental results and previous reports (Kosri et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021; Lv et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2022a), we propose the mechanism of NCDs/CCN photocatalytic conversion of xylose to lactic acid (in Fig. 8 ). Under visible light irradiation, CCN can directly absorb short-wavelength UV and visible light. Simultaneously, due to the up-conversion properties of NCDs, long-wavelength light is converted to short-wavelength light, generating more electron-hole pairs. Additionally, NCDs act as electron acceptors, capturing electrons photoexcited on CCN, significantly enhancing the separation of electrons and holes and promoting surface catalytic reactions (Jin et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022b). Notably, the ultrathin structure of NCDs/CCN allows electrons in the conduction band to quickly transfer to the surface, thereby improving charge transport capability (Zhang et al., 2023). In NCDs/CCN, e− and h+ can react with H2O and O2 to generate ·O2 −, which further produces ·OH and 1O2. Thus, the photocatalytic reaction can be summarized as follows:
(1)
NCDs / CCN + hv →
e −
+
h +
(2)
O 2
+
e −
→ ∙
O 2 −
(3)
h +
+
O 2
/ ∙
O 2 −
→
1
O 2
(4)
h +
+
OH −
→ ∙ OH

Under the influence of 1O2, h+, ·OH, and ·O2 −, xylose in the photocatalytic reaction system is selectively oxidized to xylonic acid. Subsequently, xylonic acid undergoes α-oxidation or β-oxidation to produce intermediate I and formic acid or intermediate II and oxalic acid, where intermediate I ultimately converts to formic acid, and intermediate II converts to oxalic acid and finally to formic acid. Concurrently, xylose isomerizes to xylulose, which then transforms into glycoaldehyde and 2-hydroxypropanone via aldol condensation. Subsequently, glycoaldehyde converts to lactic acid, with a yield of 87.6 %, which is significantly higher than that of other products such as formic acid.

In this study, a highly efficient composite photocatalyst, NCDs/CCN, was synthesized utilizing a straightforward dual-calcination technique. The incorporation of NCDs into CCN significantly reduced the band gap of the composite, enhancing visible light absorption and accelerating the migration rate of photogenerated carriers. Photocatalytic experiments demonstrated that, within a xylose-alkali system, the catalyst exhibited a lactate yield of 87.6 % after 90 min at a dosage of 20 mg. EPR spectroscopy confirmed that ·O2 − are the predominant reactive species. Furthermore, density functional theory (DFT) calculations elucidated the reaction mechanism and active sites between NCDs/CCN and xylose molecules. This work provides substantial support for the economic viability of producing lactate from biomass sugars via photocatalytic technology.

Xiao Cao: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation. Xin Wang: Investigation, Data curation. Yiyang He: Validation, Investigation. Jixin Li: Software, Investigation. Haiyan Yang: Validation. Pingping Zhang: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Songrui Yang: Investigation. Suyun Xu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation. Haiping Yuan: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Lin Gu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This manuscript was funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 52370144).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.141645.
